_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
min_doctor_qualification: DM/MD/DNB equivalent (in General Medicine, Endocrinology & Metabolism)
clinical_indications: Plasma glucose level greater than 600 mg/dL
Plasma effective osmolarity greater than 320 mOsm/L

preauth.label: Clinical Notes (evaluation findings, indications, planned treatment)

claim.label: Detailed Indoor Case Papers with treatment details

ppd_preauth.q: Was the Clinical Notes including evaluation findings, indications for the procedure, and planned line of treatment submitted?
Were the following Investigation reports submitted? 1. Blood glucose 2. Serum osmolarity 3. urine ketones 4. hemogram
Was the serum osmolarity of patient greater than 320 mOsm/L?

cpd_claim.q: Was Detailed Indoor Case Papers with vital (BP and Pulse) and Treatment details submitted?
Were the following post treatment investigation reports submitted? 1. Blood glucose 2. Serum osmolarity 3. HbA1C
Was the Detailed Discharge Summary submitted with the date of the follow-up mentioned?

common_queries: Serum osmolarity not greater than 320 mOsm/L or not documented at pre-authorization.
Plasma glucose level not greater than 600 mg/dL or not documented at pre-authorization.
Absence of significant ketoacidosis not confirmed for diagnosis.
Clinical notes, evaluation findings, indications, or planned line of treatment not submitted.
Missing or incomplete pre-authorization investigation reports (Blood glucose, Serum osmolarity, urine ketones, hemogram).
Missing or incomplete detailed indoor case papers with vital (BP and Pulse) and treatment details at claim submission.
Missing or incomplete post-treatment investigation reports (Blood glucose, Serum osmolarity, HbA1C) at claim submission.
Detailed discharge summary not submitted or follow-up date not mentioned at claim submission.

clinical_key_pointers: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic syndrome (HHS) is a clinical condition that arises from a complication of diabetes mellitus. This problem is most commonly seen in type 2 diabetes.
If an infectious process precedes HHS, signs, and symptoms include: Fever, Malaise, General weakness, Tachypnea, Tachycardia.
If the precipitating factor is a cardiac, vascular condition, signs and symptoms will include: Chest pain, Chest tightness, A headache, Dizziness, Palpitation.
The typical clinical presentation of patients with HHS is increased urination (polyuria) and increase water intake (polydipsia).
Weakness, malaise, and lethargy can also be part of the complaint.
Severe dehydration from HHS can also affect the skin and integumentary system. Typically, the skin and the oral mucosa are dry with a delayed capillary refill.

The most important distinguishing factor in HHS is the presence of neurological signs. Decreased cerebral blood flow from severe dehydration can cause: Focal neurological deficit, Disturbance in visual acuity, Delirium, Coma.

System-based physical assessment findings include: General appearance (ill-appearing with altered mental status); Cardiovascular (Tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, weak and thready pulses); Respiratory Rate (Can be normal, tachypnea might be present if acidosis is profound); Skin (Delayed capillary refill, poor skin turgor, skin tenting might not be present even in severe dehydration because of obesity); Genitourinary (Decreased urine output); Central Nervous System (Focal neurological deficit, lethargy with low Glasgow Coma Score, comatose in severe cases).
